headline: Common Food Compound Shown to Boost Vaccine Protection in Older Adults
meta_description: Researchers find that spermidine, a compound in wheat germ and mushrooms, may strengthen vaccine responses in seniors whose immune systems lag.
summary_short: A naturally occurring compound found in everyday foods like mushrooms and aged cheese showed promise in helping older adults build stronger immunity after vaccination.
body:
Scientists at the Max Delbrück Center for Molecular Medicine have identified a potential way to strengthen vaccine responses in older adults: a naturally occurring compound called spermidine that is already present in foods such as wheat germ, mushrooms, parmesan, and cheddar cheese. In a pilot trial involving 40 healthy adults aged 65 and older, those who took six milligrams of spermidine daily for 13 weeks after their third COVID-19 vaccine dose showed measurably improved immune responses—particularly among the roughly one quarter of participants whose bodies had initially mounted weak antibody responses. These vaccine nonresponders who received spermidine developed higher antibody levels and demonstrated stronger neutralizing activity against several viral variants, while showing reduced markers of immune system aging.

As people grow older, their immune systems become less efficient, a decline called immunosenescence that makes it harder to fight infections and respond fully to vaccines. Spermidine appears to work by enhancing autophagy, a natural cellular recycling process that allows cells to remove damaged material and maintain normal function. The compound is produced naturally by the human body but can decrease with age, which may explain why supplementation could help restore immune function in vulnerable older adults.

Researchers emphasize that larger studies are still needed to confirm these early findings and determine whether spermidine offers similar benefits with other vaccines, such as those against seasonal influenza. The supplement appeared safe and well tolerated with no adverse effects reported. Dr. Katja Simon, who led the research, notes that while many older adults respond well to vaccines, "some do not develop strong protection, even after repeated vaccination." This work suggests a simple nutritional intervention could eventually help protect one of our most vulnerable populations.
quick_facts: Spermidine found naturally in wheat germ, mushrooms, and aged cheese
40 adults over 65 tested spermidine supplements after COVID-19 vaccination
One quarter of participants initially showed weak antibody responses
Six milligrams daily for 13 weeks improved immune markers in nonresponders
Research published in Aging Cell, led by Max Delbrück Center scientists
